title: Pinwheel Circuit

sections.description: A bright little engine starts spinning before it has room to boast.
The hook learns how to travel in pairs and starts grinning at itself.
The circuit catches sidewind and the harmony starts leaning under the spin.
The machine is seen from the side: fewer sparks, more mechanism.
Everything locks back in, tighter and brighter, without losing its wit.
A single latched bar snaps the loop shut and throws it open again.

insights.message: Counterline braid returns in the braid and overclock sections.
Rotor spark repeats through the first half of the circuit.
Crosswind rotor shifts the accents without losing the spin.
Sidelight strips the rotor down to a wink and a breath.
Overclock packs the spin tighter before the latch.
201 symbolic timing placements were normalized into absolute beat events.

rhythm.summary: 100% of note events carry rhythm roles, 100% anchor downbeat presence, 0 offbeat-only bars, cadence contract satisfied
insights: Rhythmic bar roles cover 20 of 20 bars.
Anchor voice articulates the downbeat in 100% of its active bars.
Every active bar retains at least one strong-beat articulation.
Cadence contract satisfied at bar 20, beat 1.

issues.message: Arrival and cadence bars are not rhythmically weightier than continuation bars, so phrase roles may blur.
Section "pinwheel-latch" is marked as cadence but only 29% of its notes land on strong beats.

validationWarnings.message: One or more bars sustain a high amount of simultaneous note material and may feel crowded.
The phrase packs many note attacks into a bar, which may read as fussy in exposed oscillator textures.
Simultaneous upper-register intervals look harsh enough to deserve manual listening checks.
The offline render clips or comes dangerously close to full scale.
There are several simultaneous collision points, which may translate into exposed roughness.
Arrival and cadence bars are not rhythmically weightier than continuation bars, so phrase roles may blur.

suggestedMusicalEdits: Re-register or spread the upper voices at the warned collision beats so minor seconds, tritones, or major sevenths are not exposed so tightly.
Thin the densest bar or two first, especially ornamental attacks that sit on top of already-held harmony.
Differentiate statement/arrival bars from continuation bars rhythmically so returns feel planted instead of merely repeated.
Reduce overlapping peak moments or ease the loudest simultaneous entries so the rendered mix keeps clean headroom.
